digital_scam.why_this_matters: Scams and phishing attacks are engineered to look convincing — even careful people can be deceived. Acting within the first few minutes greatly limits any damage.

actions.step: Do not click any links, call back any numbers, or reply to the message
actions.tooltip: Even 'unsubscribe' links in scam emails can confirm your address is active

actions.step: Report the message as phishing to your email or messaging provider
actions.tooltip: Gmail: three-dot menu → Report phishing. Outlook: right-click → Report. iMessage: Report Junk

actions.step: If you clicked a link or entered any information, change that account's password immediately
actions.tooltip: Use a unique password you don't use anywhere else — a password manager makes this easy

actions.step: Enable two-factor authentication on any accounts you may have exposed
actions.tooltip: Even if your password was stolen, 2FA prevents the attacker from logging in

actions.step: Monitor your financial accounts for any unauthorized charges over the next 30 days
actions.tooltip: Most banks let you set up instant transaction alerts via SMS or email — this is worth enabling

cyber_threat.why_this_matters: Data breaches and cyber threats can expose your personal information to bad actors. The good news: a few targeted steps now significantly reduce your exposure.

actions.step: Change your password for the affected service or platform
actions.tooltip: Make it unique — don't reuse passwords across sites. A password manager like Bitwarden (free) makes this manageable

actions.step: Enable two-factor authentication on the affected account if not already active
actions.tooltip: Use an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy) rather than SMS for stronger protection

actions.step: Check if your email appears in any known breaches at haveibeenpwned.com
actions.tooltip: This free service checks your email against hundreds of known data breaches

actions.step: Review recent account activity for any unfamiliar logins or changes
actions.tooltip: Most services show login history under Security or Privacy settings

actions.step: If financial data may be involved, consider placing a free credit freeze at all three bureaus
actions.tooltip: Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion all offer free credit freezes — this prevents new credit from being opened in your name

physical_hazard.why_this_matters: Physical hazards can escalate quickly. Staying informed and taking a few precautionary steps keeps you and your household safe.

actions.step: Avoid the affected area until official all-clear is issued
actions.tooltip: Follow local emergency management social media accounts for the fastest updates

actions.step: Check on immediate neighbors, especially elderly or mobility-impaired residents
actions.tooltip: A quick knock or call can make a meaningful difference for isolated individuals

actions.step: If evacuation guidance is issued, follow designated routes and bring emergency essentials
actions.tooltip: Essentials: ID, medications, charger, water, a small amount of cash. Have a bag ready in advance

actions.step: Keep your phone charged and tune into local emergency broadcast (radio or TV)
actions.tooltip: Download your city's official emergency alert app if available for push notifications

actions.step: Document any property damage with photos once it is safe to do so
actions.tooltip: Timestamped photos are important for insurance claims and government assistance applications

weather.why_this_matters: Severe weather events develop quickly. Preparing even 30 minutes ahead can mean the difference between staying safe and being caught off guard.

actions.step: Identify your safe shelter location now before the weather arrives
actions.tooltip: For tornadoes: interior room, lowest floor, away from windows. For flooding: move to upper floors

actions.step: Charge all devices and have a battery-powered or hand-crank radio available
actions.tooltip: Power outages are common in severe weather. A charged external battery bank is a good investment

actions.step: Secure or bring inside any outdoor furniture, trash cans, or loose items
actions.tooltip: High winds can turn lightweight objects into projectiles that damage property

actions.step: Check on elderly neighbors or family members who may need assistance
actions.tooltip: Offer to include them in your shelter plan or help them prepare their own supplies

actions.step: Review your local emergency management agency's guidance for this specific event type
actions.tooltip: Search '[your city] emergency management' for official preparedness guidance

crime_alert.why_this_matters: Being aware of local crime patterns helps you make informed decisions about your daily routine without unnecessary fear.

actions.step: Increase situational awareness in the identified area — vary your routes if possible
actions.tooltip: Routine patterns can make you predictable. Small variations reduce risk without major inconvenience

actions.step: Ensure your home entry points are properly secured (locks, deadbolts, motion lights)
actions.tooltip: Check that door frames are solid and locks engage fully. Most break-ins are opportunistic

actions.step: Avoid leaving valuables visible in your vehicle, especially in affected parking areas
actions.tooltip: Move items to the trunk before arriving at your destination — doing it on-site signals what's inside

actions.step: Report any suspicious activity to local non-emergency police line (not 911 unless immediate danger)
actions.tooltip: Community reporting helps police allocate patrols effectively — you don't need certainty to report

actions.step: Connect with your neighborhood watch or community safety group for shared awareness
actions.tooltip: Nextdoor, community Facebook groups, or local police department community pages are good starting points

generic.why_this_matters: Taking proactive steps when a safety concern is identified keeps you and your community better protected.

actions.step: Review your account security: change passwords for any potentially affected services
actions.tooltip: Use unique passwords for each service — a password manager makes this practical

actions.step: Enable two-factor authentication on important accounts if not already active
actions.tooltip: This single step prevents the vast majority of account takeover attempts

actions.step: Stay informed via official sources and avoid sharing unverified information
actions.tooltip: Official city, county, or agency websites are the most reliable sources

actions.step: Report the incident to the relevant authority (police non-emergency, FTC, or local agency)
actions.tooltip: FTC (reportfraud.ftc.gov) for scams, IC3 (ic3.gov) for cybercrime, local police for physical incidents
